title: Skills for the future
explanation:
Skills for the future

As technology continues to change the workplace, employers are placing greater value on skills that are difficult to automate. These include communication, problem-solving, creativity and the ability to work well with others. Technical knowledge still matters, but experts say workers will increasingly need to keep learning throughout their careers. In a fast-changing economy, the most successful employees may not be those who know the most today, but those who can adapt most quickly tomorrow.

questions.text: Employers value skills that are difficult to [*automate|advertise|measure].
The text mentions communication and [*problem-solving|driving|accounting only].
questions.feedback: Problem-solving is one of the skills listed.

questions.text: Experts say workers will need to keep [*learning|travelling|changing companies] throughout their careers.
The most successful employees may be those who can [*adapt quickly|work alone|avoid technology].

exercise_sets.title: Exercise 2
exercise_sets.instructions: Choose the correct option for each gap.

questions.text: Technical ____ still matters, according to the article. | *knowledge | weather | fitness
Creativity is hard to ____. | *automate | commute | refund
Workers need to learn throughout their ____.  | *careers | holidays | complaints
The economy is described as fast-____. | *changing | sleeping | finishing
The passage is mainly about [*future employability|city transport|travel safety].
questions.feedback: It is about work skills for the future.

questions.text: The writer suggests knowledge alone is [*not enough|completely unnecessary|the only important thing].
Teamwork is implied in the phrase [*work well with others|know the most today|keep learning].
